summary: Julia Evans compiled a crowdsourced list of Linux topics people want to learn more about, gathered from Twitter responses. The list spans networking, kernel internals, process management, filesystems, security, and more. She reflects on how the list serves multiple purposes: giving her ideas for drawings, reminding her how much there is still to learn, and showing how far she's come. She encourages others to pick a topic and write about it, noting that many of these topics are accessible with a bit of research.
key_points: Crowdsourced lists of what people want to learn reveal both the breadth of Linux knowledge and common gaps in understanding
Even experienced practitioners have significant gaps in their Linux knowledge, and that's normal and exciting rather than shameful
Looking at what you don't know can simultaneously remind you how far you've come and how much is left to explore
Many seemingly intimidating Linux topics are actually accessible if you just start reading about them
Anyone can contribute to technical education by picking an interesting topic and writing about it
money_quotes: there are many things I don't know on this list and it's a cool reminder of how much interesting stuff there still is to learn!

it's also a cool reminder of how far I've come – I at least know where to *start* with most of the things on this list, even if I definitely could not explain a lot of them in detail without looking some stuff up.
you too could write interesting blog posts / drawings on the internet
"what is dmesg even doing" is an interesting topic, and totally possible to learn about!
I just read dmesg on Wikipedia and now I know more!
key_insight: A crowdsourced list of unknown Linux topics is both a roadmap for learning and a reminder that gaps in knowledge are normal, exciting, and fillable by anyone willing to start reading.
